prior.what_is_it: NREL (National Renewable Energy Laboratory) Transportation research covers zero-emission vehicle technologies, hydrogen infrastructure, SAF production pathways, and battery cost analysis. NREL provides the most authoritative US government research on clean transportation technology readiness and cost trajectories. Key tools include PVWatts (solar ROI), SAM (system advisor), and the NSRDB (solar radiation data).
prior.why_matters: NREL data is the US government's primary technology research output for transport decarbonisation. For freight forwarders: (1) NREL battery cost data and EV range projections are the most cited US source for fleet transition planning, (2) NREL hydrogen infrastructure assessments determine when H2 trucks become viable on US corridors, (3) PVWatts/SAM tools model warehouse solar ROI for any US location.
key_data: Lab: National Renewable Energy Laboratory (US DOE)
Focus: ZEV, hydrogen, SAF, battery, solar technologies
Coverage: US-focused with global technology applicability
prior.reasoning: National lab R&D signals which green freight technologies will become commercially viable.
prior.full_brief:
# NREL Transportation Decarbonization Research: ZEV Cost Parity and INFORMES Intermodal Freight Framework

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief synthesises two active NREL-led research streams relevant to freight decarbonization strategy: (1) the NREL/DOE ZEV cost-parity modelling for medium- and heavy-duty road vehicles, published March 2022; and (2) the INFORMES intermodal freight optimization framework, an ARPA-E-funded project with a January 2024 kickoff that remains in active development. Both streams directly inform routing, fleet, and supplier decisions for workspaces operating road-priority or multimodal freight.

Convention note: "the workspace" refers to the operational profile described at runtime. Claim labels (FACT, ANALYSIS, LEGAL) and inline source citations appear throughout. Items requiring legal review are labeled *Legal Confirmation Required:*

---

## 1. What the Research Found

### Stream A — ZEV Cost Parity for Medium- and Heavy-Duty Road Vehicles (NREL/DOE, March 2022)

FACT: The NREL technical report (Ledna, Muratori, Yip, Jadun, Hoehne; National Renewable Energy Laboratory, March 2022) models zero-emission vehicle adoption and energy consumption across the medium- and heavy-duty sector. The study finds that "total-cost-of-driving parity with conventional vehicles is achievable by 2035 for all MD/HD vehicle classes." *Source: Decarbonizing Medium- & Heavy-Duty On-Road Vehicles: Zero-Emission Vehicles Cost Analysis, NREL/OSTI ID 1854583, March 2022. https://www.osti.gov/biblio/1854583.

FACT: The study projects that "ZEV sales reach 99+% by 2045 and 42% by 2030." *Source: NREL/OSTI ID 1854583, March 2022. https://www.osti.gov/biblio/1854583.

FACT: The modelled scenario finds that "80% of the MD/HD stock transitions to ZEVs by 2050, reducing CO2 emissions by 69% from 2019." *Source: NREL/OSTI ID 1854583, March 2022. https://www.osti.gov/biblio/1854583.

FACT: The study identifies two viable technological pathways across multiple market segments: "battery electric vehicles (BEVs) and fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) are viable in multiple market segments, offering alternative pathways for decarbonization." *Source: NREL/OSTI ID 1854583, March 2022. https://www.osti.gov/biblio/1854583.

FACT: The report explicitly states that "disparities in adoption rate and vehicle usage between vehicle classes and between short and long-haul applications highlight opportunities for targeted policies (to reduce emissions it is critical to understand what vehicles transition to ZEV, not just how many vehicles)." *Source: NREL/OSTI ID 1854583, March 2022. https://www.osti.gov/biblio/1854583.

FACT: The study's results are described as "highly sensitive to assumed fuel prices, technology improvement trajectories, adoption decision-making, and assumptions about future freight demand and vehicle use." *Source: NREL/OSTI ID 1854583, March 2022. https://www.osti.gov/biblio/1854583.

**Methodology and scope note:** This is a simulation study using the TEMPO (Transportation Energy and Mobility Pathway Options) model. The 2035 cost-parity finding is a model output under stated assumptions, not a market price guarantee. The 2022 publication date means the model's fuel price and technology improvement inputs predate the 2022–2024 energy price volatility cycle.

### Stream B — INFORMES: Intermodal Freight Optimization for a Resilient Mobility Energy System (ARPA-E / NREL, active)

FACT: "The Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy (ARPA-E) awarded $2.2 million to develop the INFORMES, an intermodal freight modeling framework that will provide insights to decision-makers redesigning the national freight system to meet net-zero targets by 2050." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner, Quetica LLC, undated (referencing January 2024 kickoff). https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: "The project kicked off in January 2024." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: INFORMES "aims to develop a national-level intermodal freight modeling framework to support decision-making in the execution and rollout strategy of decarbonization technologies and guide efficient operations of the intermodal freight system." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: The project explores two integrated approaches: (1) "when and where low-carbon energy sources and systems, intermodal freight terminals, and emerging vehicle operations would be deployed in the near future"; and (2) "which combination of truck, rail, and domestic maritime vessel would be used to move intermodal containers from origins to destinations and where transloading between two transportation modes would occur over the transportation network." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: The tool is described as "a Python-based software tool that is easily accessible by the public users to identify equitable pathways that meet decarbonization goals at minimum cost." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: Named intended users include "federal and regional planners, port or transshipment facility operators or planners, energy system suppliers, research and development organizations, or academia." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: "Both the tool and example simulation results will be documented in journal papers and presented to the research community." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: INFORMES "was one of six projects awarded grants this past fall for modeling optimal deployment strategies for low-carbon intermodal freight networks." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

FACT: Named consortium partner Quetica is described as "contributing our knowledge of private sector supply chains and optimization modeling expertise" and as "a major contributor to the development of the network optimization modeling tool and framework to create routable intermodal networks for highways, railroads and waterways." *Source: Quetica — INFORMES ARPA-E Consortium Partner. https://quetica.com/news-and-events/informes-arpa-e/.

**Phase and forward status:** INFORMES kicked off January 2024 and is in active development. No completion or publication date is stated in the available source material.

*Specific completion date / publication timeline for INFORMES not available from primary sources as of 2025.*

Analytical inference: ARPA-E projects of this scale typically run two to four years from kickoff, placing an initial tool release in the 2025–2027 window; this is an estimate, not a sourced schedule.

**MONITORING — Re-check window: Q4 2025 and Q2 2026.** The tool outputs and any journal publications are the expected deliverables; check NREL's INFORMES page and ARPA-E project registry at those intervals.

---

## 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**ZEV cost-parity timeline — road freight**

Operational implication: For workspaces whose freight operations are road-dependent — including drayage, surface positioning of time-critical air cargo, and overland segments connecting ports to inland distribution — the 2035 cost-parity finding establishes a procurement planning horizon. If the NREL model's trajectory holds under real-world fuel price and technology conditions, road carriers operating diesel fleets beyond 2035 face structural cost disadvantage relative to ZEV-operating competitors, which affects the workspace's carrier selection calculus and long-term contract terms.

Analytical inference: The 42%-ZEV-sales-by-2030 projection implies that within the current fleet renewal cycle (typically 8–12 years for MD/HD vehicles), a significant share of the carrier market will have transitioned partially to ZEV, altering the fuel-surcharge and carbon-cost structure of road freight contracts. Workspaces sourcing road transport for high-value, time-sensitive cargo verticals — where carrier relationships are long-term and contract terms extend multiple years — face the earliest exposure to this transition.

Analytical inference: The BEV / FCEV bifurcation identified by the study matters for infrastructure dependency: BEV-operating carriers require charging corridor access; FCEV-operating carriers require hydrogen supply infrastructure. Workspaces whose routing passes through regions where one pathway is better funded may see carrier availability diverge by fuel type before cost parity is reached.

**INFORMES — intermodal routing intelligence**

Operational implication: For workspaces that manage or advise on multimodal routing decisions — combining road, rail, and domestic maritime — the INFORMES tool, when released, will offer a publicly accessible, federally validated framework for modeling where modal shift achieves net-zero targets at minimum cost. This is directly actionable for origin-destination pairs where truck-to-rail or truck-to-rail-to-maritime alternatives exist but lack comparative cost-and-emissions data to support client-facing modal shift proposals.

Analytical inference: Port and transshipment facility operators are named as intended INFORMES users. For workspaces handling cargo through US ports — particularly on lanes connecting North American origins to international destinations — the tool's terminal-level deployment modeling is relevant to understanding where intermodal infrastructure investment will be prioritised, which affects transit time and cost assumptions.

---

## 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Decision 1 — Road carrier contract structure.** The 2035 cost-parity timeline is now a publicly sourced, DOE-backed benchmark. Workspaces negotiating multi-year road carrier agreements can reference this trajectory in contract discussions — specifically, inserting ZEV transition milestones or fuel-type disclosure clauses as standard terms. This is not a compliance requirement at this stage; it is a commercial risk-mitigation move.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific contract clauses referencing ZEV transition expectations create unintended liability or constitute a representation to clients requires counsel review before insertion into standard terms.

**Decision 2 — Client Scope 3 reporting support.** The NREL finding that 80% MD/HD stock transitions to ZEVs by 2050 with a 69% CO2 reduction establishes a modelled baseline against which client Scope 3 freight emissions trajectories can be benchmarked. Workspaces supporting clients under CSRD, SEC climate disclosure, or voluntary SBTi commitments can cite this as a peer-reviewed DOE-sponsored modelling reference when describing the road-freight decarbonization pathway. Analytical inference: this is particularly relevant for clients whose Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) or Category 9 (downstream transportation) disclosures include significant US road freight volumes.

**Decision 3 — INFORMES as a future modal-shift planning tool.** The workspace should register awareness of INFORMES now and track its release. When the publicly accessible Python tool is published, it becomes an immediately usable resource for building multimodal routing scenarios for clients on US domestic corridors — ahead of competitors who discover it post-launch.

**Decision 4 — Fleet electrification sensitivity inputs.** The NREL model's explicit sensitivity flags — fuel prices, technology improvement trajectories, adoption decision-making — are the specific variables the workspace should track in annual carrier market reviews. When fuel price assumptions shift materially from 2022 baseline levels, the 2035 parity date shifts accordingly. Analytical inference: the energy price spike of 2022–2023 and subsequent normalisation represent exactly the kind of sensitivity the model flags; an updated model run using post-2022 inputs may revise the parity year.

---

## 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- "A 2022 NREL/DOE technical study modelling the full US medium- and heavy-duty sector finds total cost-of-driving parity between ZEVs and diesel is achievable by 2035, with ZEV sales projected at 42% by 2030 and 80% of the on-road stock transitioned by 2050."
- "The same study models a 69% reduction in CO2 emissions from 2019 levels under that transition scenario — a publicly available, peer-reviewed DOE-sponsored baseline for road-freight decarbonization."
- "NREL is currently developing INFORMES, a publicly accessible intermodal freight optimization tool funded by ARPA-E at $2.2 million, designed to model lowest-cost net-zero routing across truck, rail, and domestic maritime — we are tracking its release."

**What the workspace should not say:**
- Do not assert that cost parity will arrive in 2035 as a certainty. The study's own findings are explicitly "highly sensitive to assumed fuel prices, technology improvement trajectories, adoption decision-making." The finding is a modelled scenario, not a forecast guarantee.
- Do not claim INFORMES is available or has produced results. The tool is in active development as of January 2024; no outputs have been published from the available sources.
- Do not extrapolate the 69% CO2 reduction to specific clients' fleets or specific corridors — the figure is a national-stock aggregate under modelled assumptions, not a route- or fleet-level projection.

**Questions to pose in client meetings:**
- "What share of your upstream transportation spend is US road freight, and how does your current Scope 3 reporting treat the carrier transition to ZEV over the next ten years?"
- "Have your current road carriers disclosed ZEV fleet transition plans or timelines, and do your contract terms include any clauses that protect you if the carrier's cost structure shifts with ZEV adoption?"
- "Would it be useful to model your current intermodal routing against a net-zero pathway once the NREL INFORMES tool is publicly released?"

---

## 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

**Open question 1 — INFORMES outputs and actionability.** The INFORMES framework is still in development. No simulation outputs, modal-shift recommendations, or corridor-specific findings are available from the source material. The tool's actual utility for specific trade corridors, cargo types, or container configurations will only be assessable upon public release.

**Open question 2 — ZEV cost inputs post-2022.** The NREL ZEV cost-parity study was published March 2022 and uses fuel-price and technology-cost assumptions that predate the 2022–2024 energy market cycle. The study explicitly flags sensitivity to fuel prices and technology trajectories. No updated model run using post-2022 inputs has been identified in the available source material.

*Specific updated cost-parity date under post-2022 fuel-price inputs not available from primary sources as of 2025.*

**Open question 3 — Short-haul vs. long-haul divergence.** The NREL report identifies "disparities in adoption rate and vehicle usage between vehicle classes and between short and long-haul applications" but does not (from the available source content) specify the modelled parity year by vehicle class or haul length. Workspaces whose road freight is concentrated in long-haul applications may face a different timeline than the headline 2035 figure.

*Vehicle-class-specific and haul-length-specific cost-parity dates not available from primary sources as of 2025.*

**Open question 4 — Hydrogen infrastructure coverage.** The study identifies FCEVs as viable alongside BEVs, but the geographic coverage and build-out timeline of hydrogen supply infrastructure relative to charging corridor coverage is not addressed in the available source material.

**Convergent and divergent research:** The INFORMES project and the ZEV cost-parity study are methodologically distinct but operationally convergent: INFORMES models where in the network modal shift and low-carbon energy deployment occur; the ZEV study models when and at what cost road fleet electrification becomes economically self-sustaining. Together they frame the two-axis question every freight decarbonization strategy faces — which mode, and when does ZEV become cost-competitive on that mode. Analytical inference: when INFORMES outputs are published, cross-referencing them against the NREL ZEV parity timeline will be the most productive synthesis exercise for US-corridor routing analysis.

**Regulatory connections (No-Vacuum Rule):** This research intersects directly with the US EPA's Phase 3 GHG standards for heavy-duty vehicles (finalized March 2024, phasing in from model year 2027), which mandate emissions reductions across the MD/HD sector on a regulatory timeline that runs parallel to the NREL modelled transition. Workspaces whose carrier base includes US-domiciled MD/HD fleets should track how EPA Phase 3 compliance requirements accelerate or constrain the ZEV adoption curve the NREL model projects. The INFORMES tool, when available, will be directly relevant to operators navigating EPA Phase 3 compliance planning for multimodal networks.

*Note: EPA Phase 3 GHG standards are identified here as a named regulatory connection on the basis of public knowledge; no source block for that regulation was provided in this input. It is listed under New Sources Identified as a research lead, not used as a FACT source.*

---

prior.intersection_summary: NREL's commercial vehicle and fuels research sits upstream of two items in the source pool: [Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards for Heavy-Duty Vehicles — Phase 3] (89 Fed. Reg. 29440), which represents a prior-cycle regulatory output in the same technology domain NREL continues to research, and [2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy], which was co-produced by DOE — NREL's parent — and directly references zero-emission truck technology readiness that NREL programs inform. The [National Freight Strategic Plan 2025 Update RFI] signals an active federal freight policy refresh cycle; NREL research outputs on commercial vehicles and freight infrastructure are candidate inputs to that plan revision. For the workspace, the operational coupling is: NREL research → EPA/DOT rulemaking → carrier compliance cost changes → freight rate and surcharge effects on road-secondary and air-primary operations. The chain is logical but no specific NREL finding is confirmed in the source pool as having directly produced a rate or rule change.
